sections.paragraph:
-

(1) to flee from any person or thing, followed by מִן Isai. 24:18 מִפְּנֵי 2Sa 23:11 followed by לִפְנֵי Deu 28:25 Jos 7:4.- Lev 26:36, וְנָסוּ מְנֻסַת־חֶרֶב Vulg. fugient quasi gladium.-Used of inanimate things; e.g. the waves, Psa 104:7 of grief, Isa 35:10 51:11 vigour, freshness, Deu 34:7 Son 2:17 and 4:6 , in describing the evening, נָסוּ הַצְּלָלִים “the shadows flee away,” i.e. they are become long and stretched out, and as it were flee from us. Once נָס לֹו, French, il s’enfuit, Isa 31:8 compare לְ No. 4, a.

(2) to hasten, to be borne swiftly (comp. נֶחְפַּז, נִבְהַל, Lat. fugio, Virg. Georg. iii. 462), Isa 30:16.

Piel נֹוסֵס to impel. Isa 59:19, “a confined stream רוּחַ יְהֹוָה נֹסְסָה בֹו which the wind of Jehovah impels.” [Qu. as to the rendering and connection of these words.]

Hiphil הֵנִיס-

(1) to put to flight, to make flee, Deu 32:30.

(2) to take any thing away by flight, and to put it in safety (etwas flüchten), Exo 9:20 Jud. 6:11 .

Hithpalel הִתְנֹוסֵס to betake oneself to flight, Psa 60:6 comp. נָסַס No. II.

Derivatives, מָנֹוס, מְנוּסָה, נִיס.
